currentState: Arthas and Jaina end in the throne room as former intimates separated by a cursed mechanism no personal history can bridge. She can still read him, but only as the carrier of Frostmourne’s damage; he can still recognize her, but not in any way that grants her leverage over the outcome.
powerDynamic: Arthas dominates the chamber until Jaina stops treating his body as the problem and attacks the bond that keeps it above ordinary consequence. Her power is therefore indirect but decisive: not stronger than him head-on, but smart enough to change what counts as a wound.

physicalDynamics.pattern: For one breath his smile almost collapses the years between them; the next moment the map board, the mud, and the watching officers restore the full public distance.
Jaina flattens the route sheet against Arthas's board while he rests Light's Vengeance in the mud, turning the whole exchange into a contest between evidence and command posture.

Their chapter-15 tension lives in the map and in titles: Arthas only fully looks up when Jaina names the larger chain, and her body tightens when he calls her Lady Proudmoore instead of using her name.
At the gate he comes at her through churned mud with one question already in him, while Jaina holds her ground long enough to make him hear the split answer instead of the clean hope he wants.
The chapter begins with distance already charged: Jaina fixed where she answered, Arthas emptying out in front of her instead of breaking.
Their final physical exchange is almost entirely about stance: Jaina holding the staff, Arthas turning the city into order, then later looking up at her from below the wall with washed hands.
Chapter 18 removes the body and leaves the wake: Jaina riding away while Arthas exists only as the reported fact of ships, selection, and northbound motion.

The chapter restores their bodies to the same room only to prove how little that buys her: Jaina casting into the chamber, Arthas taking the force and staying upright, old intimacy erased by the distance Frostmourne now imposes inside a few paces.

What used to be charged space between them becomes failed contact space. Jaina looks directly at him and gets not recognition but proof that the blade has changed the terms of touch, speech, and harm.
Chapter 23 restores them to the same ceremonial room only to prove that the old physical charge between them has become pure failure space: every close pass is about survival, not recognition.
Jaina repeatedly has to stand long enough for Arthas to notice her work on the seam, which turns former intimacy into the thing that helps draw danger rather than prevent it.

nameUsage.jainaCallsArthas: In chapter23 his name is witness and grief, not invocation. She uses it from inside the knowledge that the man is already mostly lost.
nameUsage.arthasCallsJaina: Any direct notice of Jaina now reads as tactical recognition of interference rather than recovered intimacy.

history.event: Chapter 6 treats their prior romance as active scar tissue rather than exposition. The old bond sharpens the exchange, but does not replace the command conflict at its center.
Arthas hears Jaina's plague case through to the actionable requests, partially credits her, and still refuses the large move she came to demand.
Jaina realizes that being right about Arthas years ago and being right about the plague now produce the same useless result when he will not change course.
Jaina gives Arthas the completed field picture and then names a Stratholme source-plan built around stock, ovens, and feed points.
Arthas grants her a head-start lane without slowing his own march, locking the pair into parallel action rather than reconciliation.
Jaina returns from inside Stratholme able to prove that her method worked on contaminated stock and equally able to prove that the city cannot now be saved cleanly.
Arthas forces the gate exchange toward a clean verdict, and Jaina gives him the plain no instead of false hope.
Jaina refuses to help Arthas purge the city the instant he turns her answer into policy.
Arthas answers her refusal with dismissal rather than argument, making the break public and final under the gate.
The chapter ends with Jaina above him on the wall and Arthas below with clean hands, a visual separation that says more than any remaining dialogue could.
Chapter 18 confirms Arthas has taken ships for Northrend with a loyalty-selected force, turning the broken relationship into a pursuit problem Jaina has not yet spoken aloud.
Muradin's briefing turns Arthas from absent destination back into a person Jaina might still reach, but the cave immediately closes that possibility.

Jaina is the first person in the room to name the deeper truth: Frostmourne comes from the same hand as the plague. That reframes Arthas not as a prince making another bad decision, but as the wielder of the larger system she has been chasing.
The cave confrontation leaves Jaina with no usable personal leverage over Arthas, only the first technical clue that might matter against Frostmourne later.
Jaina reaches Arthas one heartbeat too late to stop Terenas’s murder, which fixes the whole room as rescue-after-failure rather than rescue-before-loss.
She stops trying to solve Arthas through memory or argument and instead solves for the seam in Frostmourne that makes ordinary harm matter again.
Arthas dies only after Jaina’s anti-blade work succeeds, confirming that her final leverage over him was technical and tactical rather than emotional.

trajectory: Former lovers -> institutional bruise -> moral split -> off-page northbound pressure -> chapter 21's direct confirmation that Frostmourne has carried Arthas beyond ordinary reach. -> chapter 23’s final severance in the Imperial Chamber.

stateHistory.currentState: Old intimacy turned public bruise. Arthas still knows how to sound warm, Jaina still answers him without title, but chapter 6 makes the real relationship one of truth without leverage: she brings the correct warning and he chooses a different war anyway.
Old intimacy narrowed into formal edge. Jaina brings Arthas the sharpest truth in the room and gets not belief but a lane that runs parallel to his own decision.

Former intimacy has narrowed into a report line with old hurt still burning underneath it. Chapter 16 leaves them at the gate where Jaina can finally prove she was right in part and still cannot give Arthas the answer he wants.
The moral break stands, but chapter 18 turns it into directional pressure: Arthas is no longer someone Jaina can answer, only someone whose northbound choice now bends her future movement.

Broken past repair, but still structurally active. Chapter 18 keeps them off-page from each other while making Arthas the moving consequence inside Jaina's next decisions: he has gone north with a chosen force, and she cannot stop thinking toward the same horizon.

Chapter 21 ends the last plausible fantasy that Jaina can still reach Arthas by argument, history, or recognition alone. She sees him after Frostmourne, names the blade as plague-kin, and leaves the cavern with a new practical problem instead of any surviving romantic or moral leverage over the man himself.

Chapter 23 converts the relationship from tragic impossible reach into terminal combat fact. Jaina no longer seeks Arthas the man inside the room; she targets the sword-bond that makes him function, and when he dies the emotional residue is grief without any surviving illusion of rescue.
